original_abstract:
This article examines the history of the University of the 17th November (USL), a unique Czechoslovak educational institution that existed between 1961 and 1974. Established to provide higher education to international students—primarily from the "Third World"—the university served as a strategic tool of socialist internationalism and decolonization diplomacy during the Cold War. 

The author analyzes the complex confrontation between students from Asia, Africa, and Latin America and the Czechoslovak social environment. Despite official rhetoric promoting proletarian internationalism and solidarity with national liberation movements, the arrival of thousands of "exotic" students triggered significant social friction. The study details various forms of conflict, ranging from bureaucratic hurdles and linguistic barriers to manifestations of everyday racism, jealousy, and physical violence. Key incidents, such as the widely publicized "Neckář Case" and the mysterious death of a Rhodesian student, serve as focal points for discussing the gap between ideological ideals and the reality of xenophobia in socialist society.

Drawing on archival materials from the university and state security services, the text also explores the students' political activities, particularly their surprising solidarity with the Czechoslovak reform movement during the Prague Spring of 1968 and their reaction to the subsequent Warsaw Pact invasion. Ultimately, the paper evaluates the legacy of the USL, noting that while the project failed to fulfill its primary ideological goals and was eventually abolished during the Normalization period, it left a lasting impact on Czechoslovak academia by fostering the development of fields such as African studies, Ibero-American studies, and translatology.
humanized_abstract:
This article examines the history of the University of the 17th November (USL), a unique Czechoslovak educational institution that existed between 1961 and 1974. Established to provide higher education to international students—primarily from the "Third World"—the university served as a strategic tool of socialist internationalism and decolonization diplomacy during the Cold War.

The author analyzes the complicated conflict between students from Asia, Africa and Latin America and the Czechoslovak social environment. While the government propagandized friendship with third world countries in the spirit of proletarian internationalism, the arrival of thousands of black, Oriental, or red-skinned students was a huge shock to Czechoslovak society. The author records countless disturbances – from difficulties at department stores and at canteens to language conflicts, racism, jealousy or the odd fight on the streetcar. He concentrates on the so-called Neckář Case and on the strangest death of a Rhodesian student.

The article reconstructs the history of the USL based on documents from the archives of Charles University and State Security Agencies. It also takes into account the political activities of Czech university students in the late 1960s: their surprising backing of the reformist movement in the Prague Spring of 1968 as well as their reaction to the Warsaw Pact invasion of Czechoslovakia in August 1968. The paper evaluates the legacy of the USL project: It was not able to achieve its basic ideological goals, the project was cancelled in the period of the "Normalisation" of the Czechoslovak society. In any case, it made an original contribution to the Czechoslovak scientific development of that time, having given birth to new scientific fields – above all, African studies, Ibero-American studies and translatology.
